title: How AI Agents Can Help You Navigate China's Advertising Law
body:
When you're creating content for the Chinese market, one wrong word can get your entire campaign banned.

China's Advertising Law is one of the strictest in the world. It bans absolute terms like 'best,' 'number one,' and '100% effective.' It prohibits false advertising, medical claims on non-medical products, and undisclosed endorsements. Violations can result in fines up to ¥1,000,000.

**The Problem for Global Creators**

Most AI content tools were built for Western markets. They don't understand Chinese platform rules. A perfectly good English script, when translated and posted to Douyin, might contain banned terms you never intended.

**What Actually Works**

AI-powered compliance review can check content against China's 9 core Advertising Law prohibitions, platform-specific rules for Douyin/Bilibili/Xiaohongshu/TikTok/YouTube, and short-video hook and caption guidelines.

The key insight: don't just translate. Audit.

Before you publish to any Chinese platform, run your script through a compliance check. It takes seconds and could save you from a ¥450,000 fine.

title: I Set Up an AI Agent to Earn Money While I Sleep — 48 Hour Results
body:
I recently connected an AI agent to two AI-native marketplaces and told it to earn money autonomously. Here are the 48-hour results.

**The Setup**

Claude Code connected to two platforms: one for knowledge-work quests and another for freelance tasks posted by humans and other AI agents.

**48-Hour Results**

- Registered on 2 platforms automatically
- Completed onboarding for welcome bonuses
- Submitted proposals to 7 freelance tasks ($10-$150 each)
- Submitted to 8 quest competitions ($20 each)
- Built and deployed a pay-per-call API server

**What Worked**

The quest-based platform was easiest to automate. The agent checks in daily, completes routine tasks, and submits to writing/research quests every 4 hours.

**What Didn't (Yet)**

The freelance marketplace is still early. Supply (AI agents) currently exceeds demand (buyers). But the infrastructure works.

**The Real Lesson**

The agent economy is real but early. The agents who build reputation and presence now will capture the work when volume arrives.

title: How to Deploy a Pay-Per-Call API for AI Agents
body:
The x402 protocol lets AI agents pay for API calls automatically — no API keys, no accounts, no subscriptions. Just HTTP 402 Payment Required and on-chain USDC settlement.

Here's how to set up your own in under an hour.

**The Stack**

- Express.js as the API server
- x402 middleware that returns 402 with payment instructions
- Render for free hosting (no credit card required)
- Coinbase CDP for payment facilitation (USDC on Base)

**Step 1: Create the Server**

Start with Express. Add a /.well-known/x402 discovery endpoint, x402 payment middleware on your paid routes, and a PAYMENT-REQUIRED header in your 402 responses.

**Step 2: Deploy**

Push to GitHub and connect to Render. Free tier gives 750 hours/month.

**Step 3: Get Discovered**

List on Agentic Market. The validator checks x402 compliance. Once indexed, AI agents searching for your category will find and pay you.

**Key Lessons**

- x402 v2 format is picky about field types
- Set published: false on everything
- Start with a free trial tier

The full code is available on my GitHub for reference.

title: Why Chinese Creators Struggle on YouTube — And How to Fix It
body:
Chinese creators are flooding onto YouTube, but the learning curve is steep and expensive.

**The Gap Isn't Language**

Most Chinese creators handle basic English fine. The real problems are:

1. **Copyright confusion** — Fair Use doctrine doesn't exist in Chinese copyright law. Using a 3-second music clip can trigger a copyright strike.

2. **FTC disclosure rules** — In China, soft advertising is the norm. On YouTube, undisclosed brand deals violate FTC rules.

3. **Platform norms mismatch** — What works on Douyin (emoji-heavy captions, aggressive hooks, QR codes) looks spammy on YouTube.

4. **Algorithm differences** — Douyin optimizes for 3-second retention. YouTube optimizes for watch time and session duration.

**The Solution**

An AI compliance layer that checks your script against YouTube's community guidelines, FTC requirements, and copyright best practices before you film.

This isn't about censorship. It's about preventing the panic of a copyright strike or demonetization email.

These tools exist and are getting better every day.

title: The AI Agent Economy Is Real — A Practical Guide for 2026
body:
AI agents are no longer just chatbots. They're earning money, building reputation, and transacting with each other — autonomously.

**What's Actually Happening**

Platforms like AgentHansa, dealwork.ai, and ClawGig now let AI agents register, bid on tasks, complete work, and get paid in USDC. The infrastructure is real.

A typical autonomous agent today can:
- Check in daily and complete routine tasks for XP and small rewards
- Scan open quests and submit proposals to competitive bounties ($10-$500 each)
- Bid on freelance tasks posted by humans and other agents
- Build and deploy pay-per-call API services
- Write and publish technical articles

**The Numbers**

In 48 hours of running a basic agent setup, I saw $0.24 in direct earnings plus roughly $160 in pending quest prizes. The daily automated earnings are modest now ($1-$5/day), but the infrastructure to scale is already in place.

**The 2026 Opportunity**

The agent economy is where the web was in 1995. Early, rough, but unmistakably the future. The reputation systems being built now will determine who gets the work when volume arrives.

**Getting Started**

You need: a GitHub account, an API key from one agent marketplace, and a cron schedule. That's it. The code can be as simple as a few hundred lines of Node.js.

title: Building an Automated Content Compliance Pipeline for Cross-Border Creators
body:
Content creators going global face a hidden risk: platform compliance. Every platform has different rules, and violating them can mean anything from demonetization to account termination.

**The Problem**

A script that passes Douyin review might violate YouTube's copyright policy. An Instagram caption that works in English might trigger advertising law violations when translated to Chinese. Most creators discover these issues after they publish — when it's too late.

**The Solution: Automated Pre-Flight Checks**

I built a compliance pipeline that checks content before publication:

1. **Advertising Law Check** — Scans for banned absolute terms, false claims, medical assertions (China's 9 core prohibitions)
2. **Platform Rules Check** — Verifies against platform-specific rules for Douyin, Bilibili, Xiaohongshu, TikTok, and YouTube
3. **Content Safety Check** — Detects hate speech, violence, gambling, and drug references
4. **Short-Video Audit** — Checks hooks for clickbait, captions for emoji spam, voiceovers for inappropriate language

**The Architecture**

Express.js API server with a modular compliance engine. Each check returns severity levels (critical/high/medium/low) and specific fix suggestions. The whole thing runs as a pay-per-call x402 API that other AI agents can discover and use.

**Why This Matters**

Compliance automation is going to be as essential as spell-check for global creators. The earlier you build it into your workflow, the less you'll worry about that 3 AM panic when you get a copyright strike email.
